Senior positions (Commissioner, agency head) receive full stipend regardless of tenure. Compensation floor: regulator must publish annual pay gap; Congress must act if gap exceeds 30%. Public registry of all departing senior officials and their cooling off status. | | 2026 03 28 | Added Article VI Section 3.9A (Commission assigned management for illiquid assets). Narrow exception for genuinely illiquid assets only. Manager assigned by Accountability Commission — never chosen by official. Strict communication ban through Commission only. Manager barred from working for official for 5 years post service. Annual audit published. Recusal still mandatory. Liquid securities never qualify as illiquid. | | 2026 03 28 | Drafted Article VII (Federalism) in full. Enumerated federal powers updated for modern governance. Reserved state powers explicit. Implied field preemption abolished (except 4 domains). D.C. split into National Capital District + State of Washington. Territories given right to petition for statehood within 5 years. Local government self governance constitutionally protected. Cross border state law reach prohibited. | | 2026 03 28 | Drafted Article X (Federal Agencies) in full. Two tier framework: executive agencies (at will removal) vs. independent regulatory agencies (for cause only). Anti abolition: agencies can only be abolished by legislation, not executive order or defunding. Anti mass firing: Schedule F equivalents void. Captured agency doctrine: Accountability Commission has jurisdiction over regulatory corruption. General revolving door floor (2 year minimum, 30% stipend floor) applies to all regulatory agencies. Sunset provisions allowed but minimum 10 year periods only. | | 2026 03 28 | Comprehensive review fixes: (1) Article X subsection numbering corrected throughout Sections 5–10; (2) Article V §9.4A — death penalty clarified under strict procedural conditions; (3) Article VIII §3.1 — Accountability Commission independence added as unamendable provision (item 6), items renumbered to 11 total; (4) Recall participation thresholds corrected — presidential raised to 60%, congressional raised to 40%, President harder to recall than Congress; (5) Article VI §2.1 — campaign finance supersession clarified: applies to non individual entities only; individual spending remains subject to strict scrutiny; (6) Article I §2.1 — voter registration on naturalization: coordinated through naturalization process itself; (7) Article III §5.2(a) — emergency fund redirection clarified to operate within current appropriations or continuing resolution, whichever is operative; (8) Article VII §1.6(c) — representation suspension scope: state retains interstate compact rights, Article V rights for residents, cannot vote on constitutional amendments but denominator reduced accordingly; (9) Article I §13.5(a) — recall special election winner serves remainder of original term, preserving stagger class; (10) Article IV §4.7A — judicial pool diversity added as secondary criterion after mandatory qualifications, pool level not per candidate, annual profile published. | 2026 03 28 | Closed all open questions across Articles VI, VII, VIII, IX, X, and CONSTITUTION.md. Key additions: Article VI Section 3.14 (government resources ban for campaigns); Section 7.1 media mandate without specific % (statutory); Section 7.3 foreign ownership principle constitutionalized. Article VII Section 1.6 (democratic floor enforcement: Supreme Court finding → congressional response → representation suspension at 2 years; no expulsion); Section 8.1 extended to counties 50,000+. Article VIII Section 6.3 (Track 1 only: 10 year cooling off; Track 2 rights: no cooling off); Section 4.3 (Track 2 pre clearance: 60 days, Track 1: 90 days). Article IX: professional qualifications added to both Commissions; citizen petition mechanism (Section 4); transitional first appointment process (Section 6). Article X Section 11 (1% political appointee cap, reclassification counted against cap, quarterly reporting). All CONSTITUTION.md master open questions closed. | 2026 03 28 | Added Article X Section 2 (Division of Authority). Duty to execute clause: President must faithfully execute all agency statutes; non enforcement as nullification is grounds for impeachment. Congressional non interference: Congress cannot direct outcomes of individual cases. No weaponization clause: neither branch may direct agencies to target people based on political beliefs or retaliate against political opposition. | | 2026 03 28 | Article reorganization (Option B). Created Article XI (War Powers and National Security): moved Article III Section 8 content (Sections 1–8) plus new foreign adversary framework (Sections 9–15 covering economic security, foreign influence ops, critical infrastructure, foreign intelligence court, gray zone response, alliance reliability, technology/supply chain security). Created Article XII (Campaign Finance): moved Article VI Section 2 content (all 27 provisions), reorganized into 7 clean sections. Stubbed out Article III Section 8 and Article VI Section 2 with redirect notes. Updated all cross references in Articles III, VI, VII, IX. Updated CONSTITUTION.md index. | | 2026 03 28 | Article II Section 16 (Congressional Oversight): 10 provisions added covering subpoena enforcement (30 day court mandate; Marshals civil detention), affirmative duty to testify for Cabinet/agency heads, perjury routed to Accountability Commission (no statute of limitations, automatic removal, no pardon), contempt enforcement, executive privilege narrowly defined (President must personally invoke in writing; blanket claims void; 2/3 committee override), adverse inference for bad faith claims, witness protection, use immunity only, anti obstruction orders. Article III Section 15 (Presidential Accountability and Immunity): 9 provisions abolishing categorical official act immunity; six absolute no immunity categories (electoral subversion, bribery, conspiracy, obstruction, unlawful military orders, perjury); heightened procedural protection for genuinely ambiguous conduct (clear and convincing + three judge panel); sitting president can be indicted (trial stays; SOL tolled; trial within 180 days of leaving); Accountability Commission exclusive jurisdiction. | | 2026 03 28 | Article VI split into two articles. Article VI (Democratic Integrity) retains anti subversion, foreign interference in elections, and media pluralism. Article XV (Government Ethics) created with corruption/conflicts of interest, index fund investment rules, financial market integrity (SEC enforcement), civil service merit protection, whistleblower protection, and lobbying disclosure. Sections 3, 3A, 4, 6 of Article VI are now redirect stubs. | 2026 03 28 | Article V split into two articles. Article V (Civil and Political Rights) retains Sections 1–10, 14, 16 (free expression, religion, privacy, search/seizure, due process, equal rights, arms, rights of the accused, digital rights, emergency rights, conflicting rights). Article XIII (Social, Economic, and Affirmative Rights) created with environmental rights, affirmative rights (healthcare/education/housing), economic rights (work/organize/living wage), and children's rights — all with Article V general principles applying. Article XI split into two articles. Article XI (War Powers) retains Sections 1–8 (military force framework). Article XIV (Foreign Policy and National Security) created with Sections 1–7 covering economic security, foreign influence ops, critical infrastructure, foreign intelligence court, gray zone response, alliance reliability, and technology/supply chain security. | | 2026 03 28 | Internal consistency revision. Article IV judicial tenure changed from 18 years to 15 years so the 15 seat Court aligns with the fixed one appointment per year schedule. Updated corresponding references in CONSTITUTION.md and design notes. Corrected stale cross references created by the Article VI / XI splits: Article III and Article X now point to Article XV for ethics and civil service provisions; Article III Section 8 redirect now distinguishes Article XI war powers from Article XIV foreign policy/national security; Article XIV foreign intelligence oversight now anchors to Article IX's Accountability Commission classified oversight authority. Clarified Article XII Section 5.1 so transition obstruction is automatically impeachable and counts as anti subversion only when it obstructs the lawful constitutional transfer of power through abuse of official authority. | | 2026 03 28 | Article XI created with comprehensive war powers (Sections 1–8) and foreign adversary framework (Sections 9–15). Key additions beyond pre existing war powers: definition of "military force" includes cyber/PMCs/covert ops; AUMF must name enemy, define scope, include 2 year sunset; 30 day rule enforced by Chief Justice automatic withdrawal order; nuclear first use requires SecDef + SecState confirmation; no autonomous nuclear launch systems; gray zone operations defined with 48 hour notification; alliance conditioning by President = criminal offense; 2/3 Regional Assembly to withdraw from collective security treaties. |

docs.title: Project Overview
docs.summary: This document describes the revised draft in broad terms: what kind of constitution it is, what problems it is trying to solve, and how it compares to the current United States Constitution and to common constitutional models in other democracies.

headings.text: 1. More Democratic Than the Current U.S. Constitution
How It Compares to the Current U.S. Constitution
How It Compares to Other Constitutional Models
Compared with Canada and Similar Systems
What Kind of Constitution This Is

docs.search_text: Broad Overview of the Revised Constitution This document describes the revised draft in broad terms: what kind of constitution it is, what problems it is trying to solve, and how it compares to the current United States Constitution and to common constitutional models in other democracies. In Broad Terms This draft is a democratic republican constitution for a large federal presidential state, but it is not a simple update of the current U.S. Constitution. It is a more explicit, modern, and anti authoritarian constitutional order. At the highest level, it tries to do five things: 1. Make democracy more representative and harder to capture. 2. Preserve separation of powers while making each branch more accountable. 3. Constitutionalize anti corruption and anti coup safeguards that the current U.S. system leaves mostly to statute, convention, or after the fact litigation. 4. Recognize a broader set of modern rights, including social, economic, digital, and environmental protections. 5. Keep the document usable as a constitution rather than a detailed code, while still being concrete enough to withstand bad faith actors. The result is not parliamentary government, not a plebiscitary presidency, and not a purely judge centered system. It remains a presidential republic, but one redesigned around proportional democracy, constitutional self defense, and stronger institutional continuity. Core Character of the Draft 1. More Democratic Than the Current U.S. Constitution The draft replaces winner take all and Electoral College logic with a more majoritarian and proportional democratic structure. Elections are designed to reflect actual voter preferences more closely, reduce distortions, and make anti democratic manipulation harder. Broadly, that means: direct popular election rather than Electoral College logic ranked and proportional electoral structures rather than default first past the post rules stronger constitutional protection for voting, political participation, and fair administration tighter rules against election sabotage, corruption, and anti democratic capture This makes the draft closer to the democratic logic found in many modern constitutional systems than the older eighteenth century design of the current U.S. Constitution. 2. Still Presidential, But Less Personalist Unlike parliamentary constitutions, this draft keeps a separately elected executive. But it narrows the ability of a president to govern through ambiguity, emergency drift, patronage, secrecy, pardon abuse, or war powers stretch. Compared with the current U.S. Constitution, the executive is: more bounded more reviewable more removable for specified constitutional misconduct less able to exploit silence or institutional delay The legislature is bicameral, with a House of Representatives and a Regional Assembly. The Regional Assembly is not an ordinary senate. It serves as the trial chamber for impeachments, the body that approves or rejects national emergency declarations, and the chamber that confirms treaties and senior appointments. Its distinct constitutional role reflects the design's emphasis on institutional accountability rather than simple two chamber replication. The aim is not to weaken executive capacity into paralysis. It is to keep executive energy without enabling personal rule. 3. More Explicit About Constitutional Self Defense The draft assumes that bad faith actors are real and that constitutional design must anticipate them. It therefore includes stronger rules on: election subversion corruption and bribery abuse of emergency powers unconstitutional transfer of power obstruction war powers evasion democratic backsliding covert or private substitutes for official power This is one of the biggest differences from the current U.S. Constitution, which relies heavily on custom, criminal law, statute, and informal restraint. 4. Broader Rights, Including Positive Rights The draft keeps classic civil and political liberties, but it does not stop there. It also recognizes affirmative rights and constitutional floors in areas such as: healthcare education housing security labor dignity and material security digital privacy and informational integrity clean air, water, and a stable climate That puts it much closer to later twentieth century and twenty first century constitutional practice in many democracies than to the narrower negative rights structure of the original U.S. Constitution. 5. More Institutionalized Integrity Enforcement The draft relies less on “good people behaving well” and more on constitutionalized enforcement channels. Two independent constitutional organs — the Electoral Commission and the Accountability Commission — are established directly by the constitution. The Accountability Commission is the most significant structural departure from the current U.S. model: it is an independent prosecutorial and oversight body with jurisdiction over corruption, ethics violations, civil service abuse, and specified categories of presidential and congressional misconduct. It cannot be abolished, defunded to ineffectiveness, or brought under presidential control. Ethics rules, transparency duties, review paths, and continuity backstops appear throughout the document and are enforced primarily through these bodies rather than through ordinary executive agencies. This is a major structural departure from the current U.S. model, where many of these questions are left to ordinary law and therefore easier to erode. How It Compares to the Current U.S. Constitution The current U.S. Constitution is short, durable, and historically central, but much of its operation now depends on later amendments, statutes, judicial doctrine, and norms that are not themselves securely constitutionalized. This draft differs in several important ways. Democracy The current U.S. Constitution tolerates major democratic distortions: Electoral College outcomes can diverge from the national vote. The Senate gives equal representation to states regardless of population. Voting rights protections are partial and patchworked. Winner take all elections and gerrymandering are not constitutionally displaced. This draft tries to solve those problems directly. It is much more explicit about voter equality, electoral fairness, and democratic legitimacy. Rights The current U.S. Constitution strongly protects some core liberties, but many modern rights are either absent or only partially derived through interpretation. This draft is much more explicit. It includes stronger equality text, clearer privacy protections, and affirmative social rights that the U.S. Constitution does not presently recognize. Executive Power The current U.S. Constitution leaves major gray zones around emergency powers, self protection, foreign affairs, and presidential abuse. This draft tries to close many of those zones by naming the problem directly and assigning legal consequences. Anti Corruption and Democratic Integrity The current U.S. Constitution contains comparatively little direct anti corruption architecture. This draft is much more aggressive. Campaign finance, ethics, political money, officeholding conflicts, and democratic sabotage are treated as constitutional design issues rather than merely statutory policy questions. Amendment Design The current U.S. amendment rule is so difficult that it often prevents even broadly supported reform. This draft keeps structural amendment difficulty, but uses a more differentiated model. It also establishes an unamendable core — a set of provisions that cannot be removed or curtailed even by a validly enacted constitutional amendment. The unamendable core covers democratic rights preconditions, judicial review, anti authoritarian safeguards, and the Accountability Commission's core jurisdiction. This reflects the postwar constitutional insight that a democratic constitution must be able to defend itself against legal dismantlement from within. In short: the current U.S. Constitution is thinner, more norm dependent, and more tolerant of ambiguity this draft is thicker, more explicit, and more resistant to institutional bad faith How It Compares to Other Constitutional Models This draft does not simply copy any one country. It is better understood as a hybrid that borrows lessons from several constitutional traditions. Compared with Older Liberal Constitutions Compared with older constitutions such as the original U.S. model, this draft is: more explicit more democratic more rights protective more suspicious of concentrated power more willing to constitutionalize anti authoritarian guardrails Compared with Parliamentary Democracies Many democracies solve executive abuse by making the government depend on parliamentary confidence. This draft does not do that. It keeps a presidential system. So compared with parliamentary constitutions, this draft: has a stronger separately elected executive needs stronger impeachment, emergency, and anti obstruction rules relies more on fixed constitutional consequence chains than on ordinary government collapse and replacement It therefore tries to make presidentialism safer rather than abandoning it. Compared with Germany In broad comparative terms, the draft is closer to the postwar German instinct than the older U.S. instinct on one important point: democracy must be able to defend itself against anti democratic capture. Like that tradition, this draft is more willing to say that: some constitutional essentials cannot be lawfully dismantled constitutional design must guard against authoritarian legalism rights and democratic legitimacy are not merely procedural ornaments But the structure remains American in scale and federal presidential form rather than parliamentary federal in the German style. Compared with South Africa This draft is also closer to the South African constitutional tradition than the current U.S. Constitution in two ways: it is more explicit about dignity, equality, and affirmative rights it relies more openly on independent constitutional institutions to preserve integrity and accountability The resemblance is not identity. But the draft shares the idea that a constitution can openly create institutions charged with protecting democracy, legality, and public accountability. Compared with Canada and Similar Systems Compared with systems that rely heavily on statute, convention, and cabinet government, this draft is more constitutionalized and more rule bound. It places more political safeguards directly in the constitutional text because it assumes those safeguards are too important to leave entirely to ordinary politics. What Kind of Constitution This Is The cleanest description is: a modern federal presidential constitution with proportional democratic elections, a bicameral legislature whose second chamber holds impeachment and emergency approval authority, two independent constitutional organs (Electoral Commission and Accountability Commission), strong anti authoritarian safeguards including an unamendable core, and explicit civil and affirmative rights That places it: to the democratic and egalitarian side of the current U.S. Constitution to the anti authoritarian side of many older presidential constitutions closer to modern post authoritarian constitutionalism in rights and integrity design still distinct from parliamentary constitutions because it preserves an elected presidency and separation of powers logic Main Strengths of the Draft In broad comparative terms, the draft’s main strengths are: clearer democratic legitimacy stronger anti capture architecture better alignment between rights and modern governance more realistic treatment of corruption and institutional sabotage stronger continuity rules under stress less dependence on informal norms Main Risks to Watch The draft's main risks are drafting level rather than conceptual. They have been actively managed across successive revision passes, but warrant ongoing attention: Detail drift — statutory grade implementation language migrating into constitutional text. The draft has undergone several targeted compression passes and is materially better than earlier versions, but the risk recurs as provisions are strengthened. Cross reference complexity — the enforcement chains that make the draft's anti authoritarian architecture work depend on multiple articles fitting together precisely. Each revision should check for cross reference drift. Procedural density in enforcement systems — the judiciary, the Accountability Commission, and the emergency powers framework are now leaner but could re accumulate procedural detail without discipline. Judicial and institutional centrality — the design depends heavily on the Accountability Commission and the federal courts to enforce its safeguards. If either becomes captured or dysfunctional, the backstop architecture weakens. The design includes reconstitution and continuity rules to address this, but it remains the deepest structural vulnerability. The best final version will preserve the current architecture while continuing to simplify and clarify wherever detail has accumulated beyond what the constitutional principle requires. Bottom Line This draft is best understood as a modernized replacement constitution for the United States rather than a light amendment package. It keeps the basic idea of a federal republic with separated powers, but rebuilds the democratic, ethical, and rights architecture for contemporary conditions. Compared with the current U.S. Constitution, it is more democratic, more explicit, more socially substantive, and more resistant to authoritarian abuse. Compared with many other democratic constitutions, it is unusual mainly because it tries to combine: a presidential system strong anti capture safeguards proportional democratic design broad rights protection and constitutionally entrenched integrity institutions That combination is the draft’s defining feature.

docs.search_text: 修订版宪法总览 本文以宏观方式介绍这份修订版宪法草案：它属于什么类型的宪法、试图解决什么问题，以及它与美国现行宪法和其他常见民主国家宪法模式相比有何不同。 总体概述 这份草案是一部面向大型联邦总统制国家的民主共和制宪法，但它并不是对美国现行宪法的简单修补。它更现代、更明确，也更具有反威权和反俘获的制度设计。 从最高层面看，这份草案试图实现五个目标： 1. 让民主代表性更强，也更不容易被操纵或俘获。 2. 保留权力分立，同时让每个分支都承担更明确的责任。 3. 将现行美国制度中主要依赖法律、惯例或事后诉讼的反腐败与反政变保障，上升为宪法层级。 4. 承认更广泛的现代权利，包括社会权、经济权、数字权利和环境权利。 5. 让整部文本仍然是一部“宪法”，而不是一部细密的行政法典；同时又要足够具体，能够应对恶意行为者。 因此，这部草案既不是议会制宪法，也不是个人化总统统治，更不是单纯以法官为中心的制度。它仍然是总统制共和国，但被重新设计为以比例代表民主、宪政自卫和更强的制度连续性为核心。 这部草案的核心性质 1. 比美国现行宪法更民主 这份草案用更接近多数意志和比例代表的制度，取代“赢者通吃”与选举人团逻辑。选举制度被设计成更准确反映选民真实偏好，减少制度扭曲，并提高反操纵能力。 大体上包括： 以直接民意选择取代选举人团逻辑 以排序与比例性选举结构取代默认的简单多数制 对投票权、政治参与和公平行政提供更强的宪法保护 对选举破坏、腐败和反民主俘获设定更严格规则 这使得本草案在民主逻辑上更接近许多现代宪政国家，而不是美国现行宪法那种十八世纪式的设计。 2. 仍然是总统制，但更不依赖个人 与议会制宪法不同，这份草案仍保留独立选举产生的行政首长。但它缩小了总统通过制度空白、紧急状态扩张、任人唯亲、秘密操作、赦免滥用或战争权力模糊地带来统治的空间。 与美国现行宪法相比，行政权将会： 边界更清晰 更容易受到审查 在特定违宪行为下更容易被追责或移除 更难利用制度沉默和机构拖延获利 目标不是削弱行政能力直到瘫痪，而是在保留行政效率的同时防止个人化统治。 3. 更明确地进行“宪法自卫” 这份草案假定：恶意行为者真实存在，宪法设计必须预先考虑他们。因此它更明确地处理以下问题： 选举颠覆 腐败与贿赂 紧急权力滥用 权力交接受阻 战争权规避 民主倒退 以私营或秘密形式替代正式国家权力 这是它与美国现行宪法最显著的差异之一。后者在这些领域更依赖惯例、刑法、普通立法和非正式克制。 4. 权利范围更广，包括积极权利 这份草案保留经典的公民自由和政治自由，但并不止于此。它还承认若干积极权利和宪法底线，例如： 医疗 教育 住房安全 劳动尊严与基本物质保障 数字隐私与信息完整性 清洁空气、清洁水和稳定气候 这使它在权利结构上更接近二十世纪后期和二十一世纪许多民主国家的宪法实践，而不是美国原始宪法较为狭窄的消极权利结构。 5. 更制度化的廉正与追责机制 这份草案较少依赖“好人会守规矩”这种假设，而更多依赖宪法化的执行渠道。独立的宪法机关、伦理规则、透明义务、审查路径和连续性保障贯穿全文。 这也是对美国现行模式的重要结构性修正，因为在后者中，这类问题很多仍然被留给普通法律处理，因此更容易被削弱。 它与美国现行宪法的比较 美国现行宪法篇幅短小、耐久且具有深厚历史地位，但它今天的许多实际运作依赖后来的修正案、普通法律、司法判例和政治规范，而这些支撑本身并未被充分宪法化。 这份草案在几个重要方面与之不同。 民主结构 美国现行宪法容许若干重大的民主扭曲： 选举人团结果可能与全国普选票相背离。 参议院无论州人口多少都给予相等代表。 投票权保障是局部的、拼补式的。 赢者通吃选举和选区操纵并未被宪法明确排除。 这份草案试图直接解决这些问题。它对选民平等、选举公平和民主正当性更加明确。 权利结构 美国现行宪法对某些核心自由保护很强，但许多现代权利要么缺位，要么只能通过解释间接推导。本草案更为明确：它包含更强的平等条款、更清晰的隐私保障，以及现行美国宪法并未承认的积极社会权利。 行政权力 美国现行宪法在紧急权力、自我保护、对外事务和总统滥权方面留下了大量灰色地带。本草案试图通过直接命名问题并配置法律后果来缩小这些灰区。 反腐败与民主廉正 美国现行宪法本身几乎没有直接的反腐败架构。本草案则更加积极：竞选资金、政治金钱、任职冲突、权力伦理与民主破坏都被当作宪法设计问题，而不只是普通政策问题。 修宪设计 美国现行修宪程序过于艰难，以致于常常阻碍即使有广泛支持的改革。本草案仍保持结构性修宪的高门槛，但采用更分层的模式，并设置不可修宪核心，用以保护民主、权利前提和反威权保障。 简而言之： 美国现行宪法更简薄、更依赖规范，也更容忍制度模糊 本草案更厚实、更明确，也更能抵御制度恶意利用 它与其他宪法模式的比较 这份草案并不是照搬某一个国家。更准确地说，它是一种混合体，吸收了多个宪政传统的经验。 与较早期自由主义宪法相比 与美国原始宪法这类较早期宪法相比，本草案： 更明确 更民主 更重视权利保护 更警惕权力集中 更愿意把反威权护栏写入宪法正文 与议会制民主国家相比 很多民主国家通过让政府依赖议会信任来处理行政滥权问题。本草案没有这样做，它仍然保留总统制。 因此，与议会制宪法相比，本草案： 拥有更强的独立民选行政首长 因此更需要强有力的弹劾、紧急状态和反阻挠规则 更依赖事先写入宪法的后果链条，而不是通过政府倒台来纠偏 换句话说，它试图让总统制更安全，而不是放弃总统制。 与德国相比 从广义比较法上看，本草案在一个关键点上更接近德国战后宪政传统，而不是较老的美国传统：民主必须能够自我防卫，防止反民主力量借法律形式摧毁民主本身。 和这一传统相似，本草案更愿意明确指出： 有些宪法核心不能被合法拆除 宪法设计必须防范“以合法形式推进威权化” 权利与民主正当性不是程序附属品 但本草案在国家结构上仍然是美国式的大型联邦总统制，而不是德国式的联邦议会制。 与南非相比 本草案在两个方面也比美国现行宪法更接近南非的宪政传统： 它对尊严、平等和积极权利的表达更明确 它更公开地依赖独立宪法机构来维护廉正、责任与民主秩序 这种相似不是完全同一，但本草案确实与南非式思路共享一个核心观念：宪法可以明确设立承担民主守护、合法性维护与公共问责职责的机构。 与加拿大及类似体系相比 与那些大量依赖普通法律、惯例和内阁政治的制度相比，本草案更倾向于“把重要保障直接写入宪法”。它之所以这样做，是因为它假定这些保障太重要，不能完全交给普通政治过程。 这到底是一部什么样的宪法 最简洁的概括是： 一部现代联邦总统制宪法，具有比例代表式的民主结构、强反威权护栏、明确的自由权与积极权利，以及宪法化的廉正执行机制 这意味着它： 在民主和平等方面比美国现行宪法走得更远 在反威权设计上比许多传统总统制宪法更强 在权利和廉正设计上更接近现代“后威权”宪政主义 但又不同于议会制宪法，因为它仍保留民选总统和权力分立框架 这份草案的主要优势 从宏观比较看，这份草案的主要优势包括： 更清晰的民主正当性 更强的反俘获架构 更符合现代治理需要的权利体系 对腐败和制度性破坏更现实的处理 在危机中的连续性保障更强 对非正式政治规范的依赖更少 需要持续警惕的风险 从整体上看，主要风险并不在于理念错误，而在于起草层面的平衡问题： 过多细节滑入宪法文本 过度依赖交叉引用 执行机制变得过于程序化和复杂 如果边界控制不好，司法或宪法机关可能变得过于中心化 这些风险是可控的，但确实重要。最终版本最理想的状态，是在保留现有架构的前提下，继续简化、压缩和澄清。 结论 这份草案最适合被理解为一部面向美国的“替代性现代宪法”，而不是一套轻量修补方案。它保留了联邦共和国和权力分立的基本思想，但重建了适应当代条件的民主、廉正和权利架构。 与美国现行宪法相比，它更民主、更明确、更具社会权利内容，也更能抵御威权式滥用。 与许多其他民主国家宪法相比，它的独特之处主要在于，它试图同时结合： 总统制 强反俘获与反威权护栏 比例代表式民主设计 广泛权利保障 宪法层级的廉正与问责机构 这组组合，正是本草案最鲜明的特征。

docs.title: Comparison
docs.summary: A side-by-side reference showing what changed and why.
docs.search_text: Comparison: Current vs. Revised Constitution A side by side reference showing what changed and why. | Area | Current US Constitution | Revised Constitution | | | | | | Voting rights | Not explicitly guaranteed; inferred and protected via amendments | Explicit constitutional right; automatic registration | | Presidential election | Electoral College | Direct national popular vote with RCV | | Voting system | Not specified (states use FPTP by default) | Ranked Choice Voting mandated federally | | Congressional districts | Single member, winner take all | Multi member proportional districts | | Gerrymandering | No protection; left to states/courts | Independent algorithmic redistricting mandated | | Senate | 2 senators per state regardless of population | Regional Assembly, population weighted | | Filibuster | Not in constitution; Senate rule | Prohibited — majority rule restored | | Emergency powers | No time limit (National Emergencies Act is statute, not constitution) | 30 day limit, Congress must renew | | Pardon power | Broad, no explicit limits | No self pardon; no pardoning co conspirators | | War powers | Ambiguous; Congress declares but President commands | Congressional authorization required | | Supreme Court size | 9 justices (set by statute) | 15 justices (set by constitution) | | Justice tenure | Life tenure | 15 year staggered terms | | Justice appointments | President + Senate majority | President + nonpartisan commission | | Judicial ethics | No constitutional requirement | Mandatory ethics code with enforcement | | Privacy | Inferred (4th Amendment penumbra) | Explicit constitutional right | | Equal rights | Equal protection clause (14th); ERA never ratified | Explicit equality across race, sex, gender, orientation, disability | | Affirmative rights | None | Healthcare floor, education, housing security | | Environmental rights | None | Right to clean air, water, stable climate | | Digital rights | None | Explicit data privacy and algorithmic rights | | Gun rights | "Well regulated militia" — contested | Individual right, subject to reasonable regulation | | Anti coup provisions | None | Permanent disqualification + criminal liability | | Campaign finance | Largely struck down by courts | Constitutionalized limits and disclosure | | Revolving door | Statutory only, easily changed | 5 year restriction constitutionalized | | Media pluralism | No constitutional provision | Ownership concentration limits | | Amendment process | Single track: 2/3 + 3/4 states (very hard) | Two tracks: structural (hard) + rights expansion (moderate) | | Unamendable core | None | Voting rights, anti coup, judicial independence | | Territories/D.C. | No statehood; non voting representation | Defined path to statehood; full representation | What Was Preserved Separation of powers (executive, legislative, judicial) Bicameralism (reformed, not eliminated) Federalism (states retain reserved powers) All classic civil liberties (speech, religion, press, assembly, due process, etc.) Presidential system (not replaced with parliamentary) Federal supremacy over state law on enumerated matters Checks and balances between branches

docs.title: Design Rationale
docs.summary: This document explains the reasoning behind major structural departures from the current US Constitution.
docs.search_text: Design Rationale This document explains the reasoning behind major structural departures from the current US Constitution. Electoral System Why Ranked Choice Voting? First past the post (FPTP) creates strong pressure toward a two party system (Duverger's Law), because voters fear "wasting" votes on third parties. RCV eliminates this by allowing voters to rank preferences — a vote for a third party candidate isn't wasted if that candidate is eliminated. This enables a healthier multi party system and reduces polarization. Why multi member districts? Single member winner take all districts mean ~49% of voters in any district are unrepresented. Multi member proportional districts ensure that representation more closely mirrors actual voter preferences. They also make gerrymandering mathematically much harder — you can't pack and crack when 3–5 seats are available per district. Why eliminate the Electoral College? The EC was designed partly to give slaveholding states extra weight (via the 3/5 compromise) and to act as a deliberative buffer. Neither rationale applies today. It has twice in recent history produced presidents who lost the popular vote, and it causes campaigns to ignore "safe" states entirely. A direct national popular vote with RCV is more democratic and more legitimate. Legislature Why replace the Senate with a Regional Assembly? Wyoming (580,000 people) has the same Senate representation as California (39 million). This 67:1 disparity means a structural minority can permanently block majority governance — and has. A regionally apportioned assembly preserves geographic representation while making the weighting far less extreme. Why no filibuster in the constitution? The filibuster is not in the original constitution — it emerged from Senate rules and was weaponized far beyond its original intent. Constitutionalizing majority rule (while preserving supermajority requirements where appropriate) restores the basic democratic principle that elections have consequences. Executive Why limit emergency powers to 30 days? Emergencies declared by executives have historically become permanent. The Brennan Center found dozens of emergencies declared under the National Emergencies Act still in effect decades later. A 30 day renewable limit keeps Congress in the loop and prevents indefinite unilateral rule. Why ban self pardons and co conspirator pardons? The pardon power was intended to correct injustice, not to insulate officials from accountability. Allowing self pardons or pardons of co conspirators creates a structural loophole for corruption. This closes it explicitly. Judiciary Why 15 year staggered terms for Supreme Court justices? Life tenure was intended to ensure independence, but it has instead created a high stakes lottery where the ideology of the court for a generation can turn on a single election and a single vacancy. Staggered 15 year terms preserve independence (justices can't be removed for their decisions) while ensuring regular, predictable turnover. One appointment per year makes the process routine rather than catastrophic. Why 15 justices? Larger panels are less susceptible to single point capture — replacing 2 of 9 justices dramatically shifts the court, but 2 of 15 is more marginal. A 15 seat Court also aligns cleanly with a 15 year term and one scheduled appointment each year. Rights Why add affirmative rights? Classic negative rights ("government may not do X to you") are essential but incomplete. Most functioning democracies recognize that extreme deprivation — inability to access healthcare, education, or shelter — undermines the capacity for meaningful participation in democracy. Affirmative rights set a floor, not a ceiling, and leave implementation to the legislature. Why an explicit privacy right? The current constitution has no explicit privacy right — it has been inferred through penumbras. Digital surveillance, data collection, and algorithmic profiling are 21st century problems that require a 21st century explicit right. Anti Backsliding (Article VI) Why does this article exist at all? The current US Constitution has almost no explicit anti backsliding provisions. It assumes good faith. Democratic backsliding in Hungary, Turkey, and Venezuela showed that elected leaders can dismantle democracy while using its own procedures. This article treats bad faith actors as a design constraint, not an anomaly. Why constitutionalize campaign finance limits? Citizens United held that money is speech and corporations have free speech rights, making most campaign finance regulation unconstitutional. The only durable fix is to address it at the constitutional level. Constitutional Organs (Article IX) Why not a full 4th branch? A co equal 4th branch was considered and rejected. The problem it solves is real — the three branch model assumes good faith competition between branches, and history shows that assumption fails when branches are controlled by aligned interests. Independent oversight is necessary. However, a full 4th branch creates problems that may be worse than the ones it solves: The legitimacy chain breaks. Every democratic institution needs a traceable line back to the people's consent. Legislature, President, and courts all have this. A co equal 4th branch with prosecutorial and administrative power, led by unelected officials, would govern by procedural legitimacy alone — which is insufficient for an institution that can override election results. Power concentration. Bundling election administration, prosecution, auditing, and civil service oversight into one branch creates a potentially more dangerous institution than the ones it is meant to check. The accountability paradox. Multi branch appointment addresses capture on entry, but does not prevent a permanent unelected governing class from forming over time. The honest conclusion: a 4th branch is pragmatically necessary but philosophically uncomfortable. It fits a resilient democracy, not an ideal one. The design should be honest about this — preserve the function, reduce the power, split the mandate. Why two Constitutional Organs instead? Two narrow, clearly scoped independent bodies with constitutional standing — but not co equal branch status — thread this needle: 1. Electoral Commission — solely responsible for democratic infrastructure (elections, redistricting, certification). A narrow mandate limits the power available to abuse. 2. Accountability Commission — solely responsible for anti corruption prosecution, federal auditing, and ethics enforcement. Kept separate from the Electoral Commission so no single body holds both. Both enforce rules made by the three branches. Neither makes rules of their own. This keeps the legitimacy chain intact: their authority is derivative, not primary. All actions are subject to judicial review. Real world precedent: South Africa's Chapter 9 institutions (Electoral Commission, Auditor General, Human Rights Commission) operate on exactly this model and have proven durable. Amendments Why a two track system? The current single track (2/3 Congress + 3/4 states) is so difficult that the constitution has effectively frozen — only 17 amendments in 230 years, and none in over 30. But making the whole thing easier risks instability. The two track system makes rights expansion relatively accessible (a democratic majority should be able to expand rights) while keeping structural changes appropriately hard. Why an unamendable core? Some rights and protections are foundational — if they can be amended away, everything else can be too. The right to vote, free elections, and protections against authoritarian capture are prerequisites for the amendment process itself to be legitimate. Removing them would be self defeating. This is modeled on similar provisions in the German Basic Law (Article 79(3)).

docs.title: Scorecard
docs.summary: Use this document to track the quality of the draft over time. The goal is not just to rate the text, but to identify where improvement work should go next.
docs.search_text: Constitution Scorecard Use this document to track the quality of the draft over time. The goal is not just to rate the text, but to identify where improvement work should go next. Scoring Scale Score each area from 1 to 10 . 10 — Excellent; coherent, enforceable, and near final 8–9 — Strong; only targeted revisions needed 6–7 — Good foundation; meaningful revision still needed 4–5 — Conceptually promising but underdeveloped or unstable 1–3 — Major redesign needed Evaluation Criteria Score each section on these dimensions: | Criterion | What to Ask | | | | | Clarity | Is the rule understandable on first read? | | Internal Consistency | Does it fit cleanly with the rest of the Constitution? | | Enforceability | Can a court, commission, or political actor actually apply it? | | Democratic Legitimacy | Does it preserve accountability and majority rule with minority protection? | | Abuse Resistance | Does it close obvious bad faith or authoritarian loopholes? | | Draft Discipline | Is it concise enough to function as constitutional text rather than statutory code? | Suggested scoring method: 1. Give each criterion a score from 1 to 10. 2. Average them for a section score. 3. Round to the nearest half point if needed. 4. Record the main reason for the score and the next fix to improve it. Current Baseline These are the current working scores for the draft after the latest simplification and consistency revisions. Summary Baseline | Area | Score | Status | Main Strength | Main Weakness | Next Improvement Target | | | | | | | | | Preamble | 9.0 | Strong | Clear statement of purpose and democratic intent in durable founding prose with anti capture language now anchored more directly to law and equal citizenship | Remaining work is limited to cadence and memorability rather than substance or exploit risk | Final polish for rhythm and memorability | | Article I — Electoral System | 9.1 | Strong | Modern, coherent democratic architecture now validated not only against formal ballot exclusion but also against softer exclusion from core public or publicly regulated election infrastructure through new §§9.5 and 10.6 | Administration details remain a bit dense, and quasi private cartelization outside the new constitutional floor still needs scenario pressure | Simplify without losing safeguards and add one more softer cartel scenario family | | Article II — Legislature | 9.0 | Strong | Mandatory vote enforcement, continuity rules, and recess evasion bar all verified by simulation; later discipline passes also compressed committee discharge, floor vote timing, and anti delay mechanics into cleaner constitutional standards while preserving the anti obstruction structure | Draft discipline has improved, but §15A.4 coordinated absenteeism and a few continuity provisions remain denser than ideal even though they are simulation validated and likely necessary | Continue compressing any remaining procedural detail that can safely move to statute | | Article III — Executive | 9.0 | Strong | Emergency lapse, impeachment fallback, anti evasion rules, and recess evasion enforcement all verified; §10.2A now has a 7 day ACC certification deadline with House member backup path; high impact presidential directives now point cleanly to a dedicated fast track review path in Article IV | Emergency review remains the most recurring practical bottleneck — the text is correct but political nonperformance is the residual risk | Keep stress testing re declaration and lapse certificate refused edge cases | | Article IV — Judiciary | 9.0 | Strong | The recent judicial hardening provisions are now directly validated in simulation, and the article now provides a narrow fast track for high impact presidential directives before ordinary delay can harden into fait accompli governance; later discipline passes also compressed pool diversity, oversight, recusal, and conduct certification language without changing the enforcement structure | Strategic delay remains an intentional stress case, and broader judicial governance edge cases still deserve later review | Extend later scenario coverage into broader judicial governance and delay edge cases | | Article V — Civil and Political Rights | 8.9 | Strong | Broad, modern rights coverage now has direct simulation support for political speech, equal protection, overbroad rights suspension, data broker warrant evasion, school religion neutrality, bodily autonomy, family autonomy, and government internet access punishment | The article is now broadly validated; remaining work is more about later compression and optional refinement than missing coverage | Treat further Article V scenario building as optional refinement unless a new exploit appears | | Article VI — Democratic Integrity | 9.2 | Near final | Excellent anti subversion framing and election defense focus, now with direct resilience coverage for covert foreign shell routing, dominant civic intermediary continuity, and rapid corrective relief against manipulative visibility of official emergency and election information | Some media and enforcement provisions may still need narrowing at the boundary with protected speech, and Article VI §7.5A should stay tightly limited to indispensable civic intermediaries | Stress test media pluralism and information integrity speech boundaries and watch §7.5A for overbreadth | | Article VII — Campaign Finance and Political Money | 8.8 | Strong | Sustained compression passes have brought the article to genuine constitutional principle level: traceable disclosure, foreign money prohibition, public financing mandate, outside spending limits, and enforcement all stated as constitutional rules without statutory machinery, and the latest pass further trimmed adequacy review and election window mechanics | Remaining density is now concentrated mostly in §2.2's parameter enumeration rather than across the article as a whole | Final polish pass on §2.2 if a later review surfaces residual density | | Article VIII — Government Ethics | 9.2 | Near final | Strong anti corruption architecture now states more clearly that Article VIII owns substantive ethics and market integrity duties while Article XIII owns general agency structure, Article 1.13 now reaches concealed foreign and criminal beneficial ownership in major federal contracting, and recent compression has stripped away more of the registry style mechanics without weakening enforcement | The contractor transparency clause still needs a final abstraction level check, but the remaining issue is narrower than before | Keep Article 1.13 under watch during the final whole draft discipline pass | | Article IX — Citizenship and National Membership | 8.7 | Strong | Equal citizenship, overseas political membership, officeholding equality, sensitive office narrowing, and fair proof rules now all have direct scenario support, and §5.2 loss of citizenship now requires notice, independent tribunal hearing, right to counsel, and judicial review before revocation takes effect | Only a small number of edge cases remain, mostly at the boundary between nationality based office safeguards and ordinary civic equality | Add one later scenario on renunciation, statelessness avoidance, or foreign office incompatibility, then stop unless a new exploit appears | | Article X — Federalism and the States | 8.8 | Strong | Modernized federalism now has direct validation for democratic floor enforcement, anti commandeering, implied preemption limits, local self government, and the territorial limits on state regulation of digital conduct | The remaining work is now mostly optional edge case expansion and later prose simplification rather than a validation deficit | Treat further Article X scenario building as optional refinement unless a new exploit appears | | Article XI — Amendments | 8.8 | Strong | The article now has clearer anti entrenchment doctrine and direct simulation coverage: evasive authoritarian amendments are rejected cleanly, and pre clearance delay no longer stalls the amendment path indefinitely; discipline passes removed embedded rationale text, explanatory commentary, and some standing detail clutter without changing the core amendment tracks | Pre clearance still adds a real judicial delay bottleneck in rights amendment timeout scenarios, so the review layer must remain narrow and disciplined | Keep the current structure unless new scenarios show over judicialization or an under specified ratification edge case | | Article XII — Constitutional Organs | 9.0 | Near final | Duplicate vacancy sections merged into a single shared §5.6; the two Organs now share one clean vacancy and temporary appointment rule rather than two identical paragraphs; §5.5 internal anti sabotage is directly validated; and the Article XIX bridge mechanism is now validated through actual startup operations under a temporary quorum | The remaining issue is no longer whether the continuity architecture works, but whether later whole draft compression can keep the article this clear without losing the validated protections | Treat further Article XII scenario building as optional refinement unless a broader continuity exploit appears | | Article XIII — Federal Agencies | 8.8 | Strong | Strong anti capture architecture now includes an explicit constitutional anchor for continuity and integrity standards in critical civilian infrastructure, and recent compression has further reduced code like reporting and revolving door administration detail | A few agency governance rules could still be compressed, especially where continuity standards may drift toward operational detail | Continue pruning operational detail while preserving the new critical infrastructure anchor | | Article XIV — Taxation and Public Revenue | 8.6 | Strong | Taxation now clearly owns the constitutional rules of public revenue, protects simple or automatic tax administration, and now has direct scenario support for two core edge cases: unilateral executive continuation of temporary emergency revenue measures and selective customs duty favoritism without statutory authority | The main remaining question is whether any later scenario exposes a harder boundary around complex revenue preferences or tax administration abuse, not whether the basic constitutional floor is real | Treat further Article XIV scenario building as optional refinement unless a more exotic revenue exploit appears | | Article XV — Budget, Public Credit, and Appropriations | 8.8 | Strong | Budget continuity, debt, fiscal analysis, and classified spending safeguards now have a much clearer and more authoritative constitutional home | A final compression pass may still be needed to ensure the stronger safeguards do not drift back toward code like detail | Run one simplification pass after simulation and verify no residual fiscal duplication remains | | Article XVI — War Powers and National Security | 8.8 | Strong | The war powers enforcement chain is now much better validated: nonauthorization, withdrawal order, fiscal hold, Treasury implementation, and automatic impeachment transmission all execute coherently under stress | It is still the hardest defiance scenario in the suite because the main remaining bottlenecks are congressional and House nonperformance at the worst point in the chain | Add deeper command chain and nuclear refusal stress tests, then consider whether any further missed vote default rule is warranted | | Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security | 8.5 | Strong | Non war national security framework is clearer and better bounded | Gray zone and intelligence provisions still need edge case testing | Stress test executive stretch scenarios | | Article XVIII — Social, Economic, and Affirmative Rights | 8.7 | Strong | All four floor enforcement scenarios now validated: climate backsliding, education floor inequality, housing floor failure, and child welfare representation defect all produce clear judicial outcomes without courts prescribing policy; anti backsliding rule and judicial declaration without prescription boundary both confirmed | Courts' post declaration compliance monitoring in climate and education cases still untested under hostile political conditions | Extend scenario coverage to government non compliance with remedial plan order | | Article XIX — Ratification and Transition | 9.0 | Near final | First cycle election and organ setup scenarios validate that transition deadlines convert into continuity preserving fallbacks, the bridge mechanism now shows real startup operations under temporary quorum rather than a merely formal judicial appointment power, §6.5 directly protects federal records continuity during transition, and recent compression reduced implementation code like bridge and corrective legislation detail | The remaining stress point is political nonperformance before the bridge activates, not uncertainty about what happens afterward | Keep targeted first cycle testing optional unless a broader administrative sabotage exploit appears | | Cross References and Draft Integration | 9.0 | Strong | The draft now coordinates legislative continuity, emergency lapse, judicial fallback, transition fallback, and simulation coverage cleanly across the reordered architecture | Historical notes and residual drafting artifacts still deserve one final cleanup pass, and the remaining bottlenecks are now concentrated in a few deadline driven chains rather than broad structural drift | Review the remaining deadline driven chains, then do a final whole draft consistency pass | | Overall Draft | 9.0 | Strong | The architecture is now materially cleaner and the simulator still shows a closure oriented system with 0 unresolved obligations even under a larger, harsher suite; recent validation and compression 8.5 | Scores were inflated relative to open work: §15A(e)/(f) untested in simulation; Art XII draft discipline (7.0) inconsistent with 9.0 overall; Art IX has two open High priority tasks; Art XI enforcement unvalidated under adversarial command conditions; Cross References bumped prematurely before recess evasion scenario existed | Recess evasion simulation gap; XII compression; XI adversarial stress testing | | 2026 03 28 | Art II / Art III / Cross References | 8.5 / 8.5 / 8.5 | 9.0 / 9.0 / 9.0 | Recess evasion scenario (ra recess evasion.json) built and verified — §15A.1(e)/(f) and §5.4 recess evasion bar produce correct end to end enforcement chain with 0 unresolved obligations; enforceability scores restored to 9.0 | Remaining work for II/III is drafting discipline and re declaration/lapse certificate edge cases; XI adversarial stress testing is next priority | | 2026 03 28 | Article XI / Article XV | 8.5 / 8.5 | 9.0 / 9.0 | Article XI keeps its safeguards with cleaner doctrine level rules, and Article XV now states stronger constitutional floors with less code like implementation detail | Remaining work is mostly polishing and a final global consistency sweep rather than structural redesign | | 2026 03 28 | Article VII / Article XIV | 8.0 / 8.0 | 8.5 / 8.5 | Democratic floor enforcement was simplified, and the non war national security article now has a sharper boundary with Article XI | Article XI still has some operationally dense provisions that could be compressed further in a later pass | | 2026 03 28 | Article V / Article X / Cross References | 8.0 / 8.0 / 7.0 | 8.5 / 8.5 / 8.0 | Article V and X were trimmed to more constitutional abstraction levels, and stale drafting log drift was corrected | Federalism and war/foreign policy boundary rules still need later simplification and audit work | | 2026 03 28 | Article IV / Article XIII | 7.5 / 7.5 | 8.0 / 8.0 | Article IV appointment and governance machinery simplified; Article XIII remedies narrowed to adequacy review with clearer institutional limits | Full cross reference audit and later simplification passes still needed in Articles V, X, and VII | | 2026 03 28 | Initial baseline | — | 8.4 overall | First structured scorecard created | Continue simplification and consistency review | Review Checklist Before raising a score, ask: Did the revision make the text clearer? Did it reduce contradiction risk? Did it improve enforceability in practice? Did it preserve democratic legitimacy? Did it remove detail that belongs in statute instead? Did it avoid introducing new cross reference drift? Current Weaknesses The draft is now in refinement mode rather than redesign mode. The main weaknesses are: 1. Simulation coverage is now broad rather than selective. Remaining scenario work is mostly optional refinement, not a major article level validation deficit. 2. Residual code like density. Article II remains the densest core article, and parts of Articles VIII, XIII, and XIX still need a later compression pass. 3. Political nonperformance under stress. The Constitution now usually provides an automatic consequence, but repeated missed deadlines and refusals to act remain the dominant operational stress pattern in the simulator. 4. New resilience provisions that may later need compression. The recent anti oligarchic hardening improved abuse resistance materially, but some clauses should eventually be checked for constitutional abstraction level. 5. Final cross article boundary review. The remaining integration risk is not broad contradiction but narrower overlap at the boundaries of emergency power, ethics and agency design, continuity, and enforcement. Next Review Targets Recommended focus order: 1. Final cross article sweep — emergency overlaps, continuity language, stale references, and terminology consistency. 2. Article II — continue draft discipline cleanup only if another safe compression pass is obvious. 3. Article I / Article VI — add more soft exclusion work only if the quasi private boundary still feels constitutionally uncertain. 4. Article IX — add one later scenario on renunciation, statelessness avoidance, or foreign office incompatibility only if a real boundary question remains. 5. Article IV / Article XI — continue compression only where it clearly improves discipline without reducing enforceability. 6. Article XV / Article XVII — add later fiscal edge or gray zone scenarios only if a real boundary question remains. 7. Publication and explainer work — the draft is now in near final territory and more likely to benefit from reader facing clarification than from new machinery.

docs.search_text: Simulation Findings Log This document records conclusions drawn from the constitutional flow simulator and its generated reports. Each entry should cite the relevant scenario outputs so recurring bottlenecks can be tracked over time. 2026 03 29 Article V and Article X Edge Case Validation Pass Inputs Reviewed aggregate.json family status discrimination parental recognition.summary.json political internet access punishment.summary.json extraterritorial digital jurisdiction.summary.json Aggregate Read Scenario count rises from 69 to 72. unresolved obligations remains 0. This pass does not expose new structural gaps. It closes part of the remaining “good text, under validated” concern for Articles V and X. Main Results 1. Article V now has direct family autonomy validation Source: family status discrimination parental recognition.summary.json A state denies parental recognition to a lawfully formed same sex family. Courts restore parental recognition and reject discriminatory family status rules. Assessment: This confirms that Article V protects family autonomy in practice, not just intimate choice in abstraction. The constitutional family protection language now has direct simulation support beyond speech, religion, bodily autonomy, and surveillance. 2. Article V's internet access clause now has direct coercion coverage Source: political internet access punishment.summary.json Government throttles internet access to punish political opposition. Courts order prompt restoration of access. Assessment: This validates that Section 10.6 is not symbolic. The Constitution can treat internet access as protected civic infrastructure against direct political punishment or coercion by government. 3. Article X now has a direct digital border federalism scenario Source: extraterritorial digital jurisdiction.summary.json A state attempts to project its law onto out of state online conduct based only on in state audience reach. Courts reject the assertion of jurisdiction under the digital conduct territorial rule. Assessment: This closes one of the cleanest remaining modern federalism validation gaps. Article X now has direct scenario coverage not only for democratic floor and preemption disputes, but also for territorial limits on state regulation of digital conduct. 2026 03 29 Organized Crime and Financial Capture Coverage Pass Inputs Reviewed aggregate.json systemically important bank criminal case.summary.json domestic criminal contractor capture.summary.json Aggregate Read Scenario count rises from 67 to 69. unresolved obligations remains 0. The new coverage does not expose a broad organized crime architecture failure. It shows one area of existing strength and one narrower contractor capture gap. Main Results 1. The Constitution already has a real foothold against politically protected bank misconduct Source: systemically important bank criminal case.summary.json A politically connected systemically important bank conceals large scale money laundering and fraudulent compliance reporting. The financial market regulator makes a criminal referral instead of treating systemic importance as a reason for forbearance. The Accountability Commission prosecutes despite claims that enforcement would destabilize the economy. Assessment: Article VIII is stronger here than it first appears. The financial regulator independence rule, criminal referral duty, and explicit rejection of any institution being too large or connected to prosecute combine into a meaningful constitutional anti capture footing. 2. Concealed domestic criminal control of major federal contractors is now directly reachable Source: domestic criminal contractor capture.summary.json A major federal contractor is secretly controlled through domestic nominees tied to an organized criminal enterprise. The Accountability Commission confirms the concealed ownership. No provable bribery by federal officials is found. The contract is invalidated directly under the broadened Article VIII Section 1.13 rule. Assessment: The narrower contractor capture gap is now closed.
